incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Louisiana (approximately 50 miles offshore)
incident__event__summary: On April 20, 2010, a blowout occurred at the Macondo oil well approximately 50 miles off the coast of Louisiana during temporary well-abandonment activities on the Deepwater Horizon drilling rig. Control of the well was lost, resulting in uncontrolled release of oil and gas. The hydrocarbons found an ignition source on the rig, resulting in explosions and fire that led to 11 deaths, 17 serious physical injuries, evacuation of 115 individuals, sinking of the Deepwater Horizon, and massive marine and coastal damage from approximately 4 million barrels of released hydrocarbons.

A fire erupted when a metal tube within a fired heater ruptured during the initial startup of a renewable diesel hydroprocessing unit. A lack of process flow due to valve misalignment combined with afterburning due to poor combustion conditions caused overheating of the tubes leading to the rupture and fire. Over 200,000 pounds of renewable diesel and 2,200 pounds of hydrogen were released. One employee received third-degree burns over 80 percent of his body and remained in critical condition for over six months. The incident resulted in approximately $350 million in damage.

On July 17, 2001, an explosion occurred at the Motiva Enterprises refinery in Delaware City, Delaware when a spark from a contractor's cutting torch ignited flammable vapors emitted from a corroded sulfuric acid storage tank. The incident resulted in one fatality, eight injuries, and the release of 264,000 gallons of spent sulfuric acid. Approximately 99,000 gallons reached the Delaware River, causing significant environmental damage and killing aquatic life. Investigation revealed that the tank had not been inspected internally or externally for seven years and had significant corrosion in the shell and holes in the roof.

On January 22, 2018, a blowout and rig fire occurred at Pryor Trust 0718 gas well number 1H-9 in Pittsburg County, Oklahoma, resulting in five worker fatalities. The workers were inside the driller's cabin on the rig floor and died from thermal burn injuries and smoke and soot inhalation. The blowout occurred approximately three-and-a-half hours after removing drill pipe out of the well. The incident was caused by the failure of both the primary barrier (hydrostatic pressure from drilling mud) and the secondary barrier (human detection of influx and activation of the blowout preventer).

The CSB reviewed and investigated numerous incidents where consequences escalated following a loss of containment due to the lack of effective remote isolation equipment. These incidents resulted in serious injuries, fatalities, environmental contamination, and severe damage to facilities. Following the 2019 TPC Group incident in Port Neches, TX, the CSB concluded that improved requirements for remote isolation are necessary and that although guidance has been available for many years, it has not been consistently followed.

On January 29, 2020, the Wendland 1-H well experienced a loss of well control that resulted in a blowout during the process of installing a new tubing head as part of a workover operation. Within seconds, the uncontrolled release of oil and gas found an ignition source resulting in a flash fire that fatally injured three workers and seriously injured another. The incident also resulted in property damage estimated to be greater than $1 million.

On April 8, 2021, a flammable liquid mixture and its vapors became pressurized and released through the seal of a closed manway of an operating kettle at the Yenkin-Majestic OPC Polymers resin plant. The vapor spread throughout the facility, forming a flammable cloud which found an ignition source and exploded resulting in a fire. One employee was fatally injured, eight others were injured including severe burns and limb fractures. The facility was demolished and firefighting water runoff contaminated nearby Alum Creek.

On February 7, 2010, an explosion occurred during the planned cleaning of new fuel gas piping at Kleen Energy, a combined-cycle natural gas power plant under construction in Middletown, Connecticut. Workers were conducting a 'gas blow' whereby natural gas was forced through piping at high volume and pressure to remove debris. The natural gas and debris were vented into a congested outdoor area where the natural gas accumulated and found an ignition source, resulting in an explosion. The explosion resulted in six fatalities and injured at least 50 other personnel.

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Pressure Vessel Rupture
incident__event__summary: On December 7, 2009, a 50-foot pressure vessel used to manufacture synthetic crystals ruptured due to internal stress corrosion cracking at the NDK Crystal facility in Belvidere, Illinois. The vessel, located in an enclosed building, generated several projectiles when it ruptured that resulted in one fatality and one injury to members of the public, as well as significant property damage to the NDK Crystal facility and an adjacent business. The vessel operated at pressures greater than 10,000 psig and was made with SA-723 Grade 2 steel. The ASME Code sections applicable to the vessel did not include specific limitations of material thickness for heavy pressure vessels.

On January 9, 2014, aboveground storage tanks at Freedom Industries in Charleston, West Virginia, leaked approximately 10,000 gallons of Shurflot 944 (a mixture of crude MCHM and polyglycol ethers) through two small holes in the tank floor. The secondary containment dike wall had cracks and holes from disrepair that allowed tank contents to escape and reach the Elk River. The chemicals flowed downstream to the intake of the West Virginia American Water treatment facility, about 1.5 miles away. WVAW issued a Do Not Use order affecting approximately 300,000 residents across nine counties.

On April 3, 2017, a pressure vessel (Semi-Closed Receiver) catastrophically failed at Loy-Lange Box Company in St. Louis, Missouri, causing a BLEVE. The bottom head failed due to oxygen pitting corrosion and generalized corrosion that thinned the vessel wall. The explosion fatally injured one Loy-Lange employee and the launched vessel crashed through a nearby building roof, killing three members of the public. The incident resulted from deficiencies in operations and process safety practices that failed to prevent corrosion, an inadequate 2012 repair by Kickham Boiler that left damaged material in place, and acceptance of the non-conforming repair by Arise Inc.'s inspector.

On August 31, 2017, following Hurricane Harvey flooding that caused loss of power and refrigeration systems at the Arkema Crosby facility, organic peroxide products stored in a refrigerated trailer decomposed and burned. Twenty-one people sought medical attention from exposure to fumes. Over several days, eight more trailers burned in a second fire and controlled burn, consuming over 350,000 pounds of organic peroxide. More than 200 residents were evacuated for a week.

On October 21, 2016, a chemical release occurred at the MGPI Processing, Inc. plant in Atchison, Kansas when a chemical delivery truck owned and operated by Harcros Chemicals, Inc. was inadvertently connected to a tank containing incompatible material. The chemical reaction generated a plume that led to a shelter-in-place order for thousands of residents and at least 120 people seeking medical attention.

On August 27, 2020, Category 4 Hurricane Laura caused severe damage to multiple buildings storing trichloroisocyanuric acid (TCCA) at the Bio-Lab facility in Westlake, Louisiana. Rainwater contacted the exposed TCCA, initiating a chemical reaction and decomposition. The resulting heat started a fire and released a large plume of hazardous gases including toxic chlorine over the nearby community. No injuries were reported. The facility underwent approximately $250 million in redesign and reconstruction before resuming operations in March 2023.

On January 28, 2021, liquid nitrogen overflowed from an immersion freezer at Foundation Food Group facility in Gainesville, GA while two maintenance employees were troubleshooting the equipment. The liquid nitrogen quickly vaporized and created an oxygen deficient atmosphere. Two maintenance employees were fatally injured. During rescue attempts, four additional employees were fatally injured and three more employees plus one firefighter were seriously injured from oxygen deprivation. The incident resulted in an estimated $1.95 million in damage.

On January 24, 2020, a leak of propylene from a high velocity oxygen fuel (HVOF) thermal spray process resulted in an explosion at the Watson Grinding facility in Houston, Texas. The leak was caused by a disconnected hose inside one of the facility's coating booths. The explosion fatally injured two employees and a member of the public, with two additional employees injured. The explosion damaged hundreds of nearby structures, including homes and several businesses.

On March 21, 2011, an electric arc furnace exploded at Carbide Industries in Louisville, Kentucky. Two workers sustained fatal injuries after hot gases and debris blown from the furnace broke through the double-pane reinforced glass window of the control room, which was located only 12 feet from the furnace. A third worker also sustained injuries. The investigation found that furnace overpressure events occurred a few times a year but were not investigated for root cause. Water leaks into the furnace could contribute to overpressure events, but Carbide did not have a robust inspection and mechanical integrity program. The close proximity of the control room to the furnace was not addressed despite previous overpressure events breaking control room windows.

On April 12, 2004, during first full-scale production of triallyl cyanurate at MFG Chemical's Callahan Road Plant in Dalton, Georgia, a runaway chemical reaction over-pressurized a 4000-gallon reactor, releasing highly toxic and flammable allyl alcohol and toxic allyl chloride into the community. More than 200 families were evacuated. One employee received chemical burns and 154 people, including 15 emergency responders, required decontamination and treatment. Emergency response was hampered by inadequate procedures, lack of protective equipment for police, unauthorized reentry of MFG personnel, and uncoordinated incident command.

On July 31, 2024, anhydrous ammonia was discharged from a pressure relief device in the refrigeration system at the Cuisine Solutions facility in Sterling, VA. During the evacuation, workers were exposed to ammonia and four were seriously injured.

On May 31, 2017, an explosion and fire occurred at the Didion Milling facility in Cambria, Wisconsin. The fire spread throughout the facility resulting in multiple combustible dust explosions both within and external to the production equipment. Five employees were fatally injured and 14 were injured. Fatalities and injuries were caused by exposure to flames from combustible dust explosions and flash fires, and building collapse. The incident resulted in an estimated $15.37 million in property damage. The CSB investigation found ineffective combustible dust safety management, ineffective process safety leadership, and lack of regulatory coverage for combustible dusts.

incident__context__operator: E. I. du Pont de Nemours (DuPont)
incident__event__summary: On November 15, 2014, nearly 24,000 pounds of methyl mercaptan was released inside the Lannate unit at the DuPont chemical manufacturing facility in La Porte, Texas. The release resulted in the fatalities of four employees (three operators and a shift supervisor) inside the Lannate manufacturing building. All four victims died from a combination of asphyxia and acute exposure to toxic chemicals including methyl mercaptan. Three victims were located on the third floor and one was descending the stairs between the third and second floor.

Three separate chemical incidents occurred from October 2021 to June 2024 at the Honeywell facility in Geismar, Louisiana, involving releases of anhydrous hydrogen fluoride from the HFC-245fa unit. The first two incidents occurred during reactor startup and the third during planned maintenance work. One worker was fatally injured in the first incident, another was seriously injured in the third. The second incident resulted in substantial property damage and a release of over 800 lbs. of HF and over 1,600 lbs. of chlorine gas when a reboiler exploded.

incident__event__incident_type: Fire during hot work in confined space
incident__event__summary: On September 21, 2020, during a planned shutdown at Evergreen Packaging's paper mill in Canton, North Carolina, a fire started inside an upflow tower when an electric heat gun being used to warm resin fell into a five-gallon bucket containing flammable resin. Two contractors working inside connected process vessels (upflow and downflow towers) in a pulp bleaching unit were fatally injured. The incident occurred during simultaneous maintenance operations by two contract companies.

On April 17, 2013, an explosion and fire occurred at the West Fertilizer Company (WFC), a fertilizer blending, retail, and distribution facility in West, Texas. The violent detonation of fertilizer grade ammonium nitrate (FGAN) fatally injured 12 emergency responders and three members of the public. Local hospitals treated more than 260 injured victims, many of whom required hospital admission. The blast completely destroyed the WFC facility and caused widespread damage to more than 150 offsite buildings.

Between January and May 2011, three iron dust-related incidents occurred at Hoeganaes Corporation in Gallatin, Tennessee. The first flash fire killed two workers, the second injured one, and the third incident - a hydrogen explosion with resulting iron dust flash fires - killed three and injured two. CSB investigation found inadequate fire department inspections despite the facility being covered by IFC 2006 which incorporates NFPA combustible dust standards. Only three inspections occurred in 12 years prior to the third accident, with no mention of combustible dust hazards despite previous incidents.

On May 1, 2002, a fire erupted at the Third Coast Industries facility near Friendswood, Texas. The facility blended and packaged motor oils, hydraulic oils, and other highly flammable liquids. Firefighters were unable to control the fire, which burned for 24 hours and consumed 1.2 million gallons of combustible and flammable liquids, destroying the entire site. The facility lacked fire detection equipment, was unstaffed at night, and had no water source for fire suppression. One hundred residents were evacuated and a local school was closed.

On October 9, 2012, a flash fire at the US Ink/Sun Chemical Corporation ink manufacturing facility in East Rutherford, New Jersey, injured seven workers, including three with third-degree burns. During start-up of a new dust collection system, a flammable mixture of hydrocarbons and combustible dusts accumulated in the ductwork and ignited. The CSB investigation found that improper design and operation of the dust collection system was a causal factor, and that minimum design criteria from NFPA standards had not been followed because the New Jersey Uniform Construction Code exempted process equipment from these requirements.

Between 1980 and 2005, 281 combustible dust incidents occurred across 44 states in various industries, resulting in 119 worker deaths and 718 injuries along with extensive property damage. After investigating three catastrophic dust explosions that killed 14 workers in 2003, the CSB conducted a comprehensive study and recommended OSHA issue a combustible dust standard. Despite regulatory enforcement and educational efforts, OSHA never issued the standard and abandoned rulemaking in 2017. The recommendation was superseded by a new recommendation from the 2023 Didion Milling Company investigation.

Three accidents occurred over a 33-hour period at DuPont's Belle, West Virginia plant starting January 22, 2010. First, 2,000 pounds of methyl chloride leaked unnoticed for five days. The next morning, an oleum pipe leak produced a fuming cloud of sulfur trioxide. Later that day, a phosgene release from a burst hose exposed a worker who died the following evening. Investigation found common deficiencies in multiple safety management systems and identified that the PTFE-lined braided stainless steel hose was susceptible to phosgene permeability and corrosion.

On January 7, 2010, a graduate student in the Chemistry and Biochemistry Department at Texas Tech University was severely injured when the chemical he was working with unexpectedly detonated. The CSB investigation found that the physical hazards of the student's research were not effectively assessed and controlled, and that comprehensive hazard evaluation guidance for research laboratories did not exist.

On December 9, 2010, a metal dust explosion and fire occurred at the AL Solutions, Inc. facility in New Cumberland, West Virginia, resulting in three employee fatalities and one contractor injury. The incident ultimately caused the shutdown of the plant. The CSB investigation found that AL Solutions did not follow the requirements of NFPA 484: Standard for Combustible Metals to control combustible metal dust hazards and did not sufficiently train employees in combustible metal dust hazards.

On March 23, 2005, a series of explosions and fire occurred at the BP Texas City refinery during the re-starting of an isomerization (ISOM) unit, killing 15 workers, injuring 180, and resulting in substantial economic losses. The event was triggered when a raffinate splitter tower was overfilled during startup. The overfilling caused pressure relief devices to open and dump flammable liquid into a blowdown drum fitted with a stack without a flare. The blowdown drum also overfilled, releasing flammable liquid out of the stack into the surrounding area where it evaporated as it fell to ground. Subsequent ignition of the flammable vapors led to the explosions and fire.

incident__event__incident_type: Multiple Fatal Explosions at Unmanned Storage Facilities
incident__event__summary: Between October 2009 and early 2010, three separate fatal explosions occurred at unmanned oil and gas storage tank facilities in Mississippi, Oklahoma, and Texas, killing four people and seriously injuring others. All victims were 25 years or younger. The CSB investigation identified 26 similar incidents between 1983 and 2010 resulting in 44 fatalities and 25 injuries. All incidents involved rural unmanned sites lacking fencing and hazard signage. The CSB found that existing API standards did not adequately address security measures for upstream exploration and production facilities to prevent unauthorized public access.

On November 5, 2005, two contractors were overcome and killed by nitrogen gas which displaced oxygen while working near a hydrocracker unit reactor at the Valero refinery in Delaware City, Delaware. One contractor tried to retrieve duct tape that had fallen inside a nitrogen-purged reactor and was overcome by the oxygen-deficient atmosphere. His co-worker attempted rescue and also succumbed to oxygen deprivation.

On July 20, 2003, chlorine gas was released from Honeywell's refrigerant manufacturing plant in Baton Rouge, Louisiana, resulting in hospitalization of four workers and requiring nearby residents to shelter in place. Chlorine entered the control room through the HVAC system, forcing employee evacuation before the leak could be stopped, and corroded the process control system rendering it inoperable. Two subsequent incidents occurred: on July 29, 2003, a worker was fatally injured by antimony pentachloride exposure from a gas cylinder release, and on August 13, 2003, two workers were exposed to hydrofluoric acid with one hospitalized. The CSB investigation identified lacking guidance and standards for positive pressure control room design and maintenance in the U.S. chemical industry.

On April 8, 2011, an explosion and fire occurred in a leased magazine at Waikele Self Storage in Waipahu, Hawaii that was being used by Donaldson Enterprises, Inc. (DEI) for seized fireworks storage and disposal-related activities. As a result of the explosion and fire, five DEI personnel were fatally injured and a sixth sustained minor injuries. The CSB investigation identified a gap in regulatory guidance and consensus standards pertaining to fireworks disposal or disassembly activities.

On November 12, 2008, an aboveground storage tank containing liquid fertilizer catastrophically failed at the Allied Terminals distribution facility in Chesapeake, VA. The incident critically injured two contract workers. At least 200,000 gallons of fertilizer were released and some reached the nearby Elizabeth River. Three other tanks with similar hazards were subsequently evaluated and ultimately demolished.

On November 17, 2003, chlorine gas was released from the DPC Enterprises chlorine repackaging facility in Glendale, Arizona. Fourteen people, including ten police officers, required treatment for chlorine exposure. The release occurred when chlorine vapors from a rail car unloading operation escaped from a scrubbing system designed to capture the material and produce bleach. The release was caused by exhaustion of absorbent chemicals in the scrubber and a back-reaction that released chlorine gas in large quantities.

incident__context__operator: E.I. DuPont de Nemours and Co.
incident__event__summary: On November 9, 2010, one contract worker was killed and another was injured in an explosion at the DuPont Yerkes facility in Buffalo, New York. Contractors were welding atop a 10,000 gallon slurry tank when hot sparks ignited flammable vinyl fluoride vapor that had accumulated inside the tank, triggering an explosion. The investigation found multiple deficiencies in the hot work permitting process, including incomplete permits, lack of hazard verification, failure to isolate the tank from adjacent in-service tanks via an overflow line, and absence of atmospheric testing for flammable vapor.

On October 29, 2003, a series of explosions fueled by accumulated aluminum dust severely burned two workers and injured a third at the Hayes Lemmerz aluminum wheel manufacturing plant in Huntington, Indiana. One of the severely burned workers subsequently died. The explosions caused property damage to the facility.

A fire and explosion occurred at the Partridge-Raleigh Oilfield in Raleigh, MS, on June 5, 2006. Three contractor workers from Stringer's Oilfield Services, Inc., died and another was seriously injured. The workers were preparing to weld pipes attached to oil production tanks. Welding sparks ignited flammable vapors from inside two adjacent tanks which led to an explosion. Key findings found a lack of safe work procedures for hot work and working on elevated surfaces.

On June 14, 2006, workers were heating and mixing flammable solvents in an open top tank in the mixing room at the Universal Form Clamp Company facility in Bellwood, IL. During the heating process, there was a sudden evolution of flammable vapors that ignited, resulting in an explosion and fire. One worker died and five others were injured.

On February 7, 2003, an explosion and fire occurred inside a vent collection system at the Technic plating chemicals manufacturing and research facility in Cranston, Rhode Island. The explosion was likely initiated when an employee tapped a small hammer against a duct that sounded blocked. The CSB concluded the explosion resulted from a violent chemical reaction inside the vent collection system, likely caused by either a violent reaction between two incompatible materials or a violent reaction of a shock-sensitive material. One employee was critically injured and eighteen others were sent to the hospital. The surrounding community was evacuated and facility operations were interrupted for several weeks.

On January 30, 2007, a propane explosion at the Little General Store in Ghent, West Virginia, killed four people (two propane service technicians and two emergency responders) and injured six others. A junior propane service technician was transferring liquid propane from an existing tank to a newly installed tank when he removed a plug from the existing tank's liquid withdrawal valve, causing liquid propane to spray out. A large vapor cloud formed and expanded into the store. The technician called his supervisor and then 911, but minutes after emergency responders arrived, the explosion occurred. The explosion leveled the store, destroyed a responding ambulance, and damaged nearby vehicles.

CSB investigation into chlorine railcar unloading operations identified the need for remotely operated emergency isolation devices to quickly isolate leaks in flexible hoses or piping components. The investigation emphasized that well-maintained, reliable emergency shutdown systems can significantly reduce potential for significant chlorine releases caused by failures in transfer connections. CSB recommended DOT expand regulatory coverage to include chlorine railcar unloading operations with specific requirements for emergency isolation systems. DOT determined it lacks statutory authority to regulate unloading operations at fixed facilities. OSHA subsequently clarified that Chlorine Institute guidance requiring automatic shutoff valves is considered RAGAGEP under PSM regulations.

On May 4, 2009, an explosion and fire occurred at the Veolia ES Technical Solutions LLC facility in West Carrollton, Ohio. Tetrahydrofuran (THF), a flammable organic solvent, was released from a solvent recovery process in a tank farm area. Uncontrolled venting of THF allowed flammable vapors to accumulate to explosive concentrations outside the process equipment. The gas subsequently ignited, likely from two natural gas-fired boilers in a lab/operations building located 30 feet away. The initial blast injured four workers, two seriously. Multiple subsequent explosions significantly damaged the site, and nearby residences and businesses also sustained considerable damage.

On January 25, 2005, an explosion occurred at Acetylene Services Company (ASCO) in Perth Amboy, NJ. Acetylene produced in a generator flowed back past a check valve through a recycled water line and accumulated in a shed, where it ignited and exploded. The check valve, supplied by Rexarc International, Inc., was susceptible to malfunction due to its design.

On August 19, 2004, an explosion occurred at the Sterigenics International ethylene oxide sterilization facility in Ontario, California. Plant maintenance personnel bypassed safety devices, allowing flammable ethylene oxide to travel through the ventilation system to an open-flame catalytic oxidizer (emissions control device), triggering a large explosion. The facility had more than 5,000 lbs of ethylene oxide on site and was subject to OSHA Process Safety Management standards.

incident__event__incident_type: Hydrocarbon Release and Fire with Secondary HF Release
incident__event__summary: On July 19, 2009, a hydrocarbon gas release occurred at the CITGO Corpus Christi East Refinery HF alkylation unit when violent shaking in process recycle piping broke two threaded connections. The shaking was caused by nearly complete flow blockage due to sudden failure of a control valve whose internal plug unthreaded from the valve stem. The hydrocarbon cloud ignited causing extensive damage and a multi-day fire. One employee was critically injured and another treated for possible HF exposure. The fire caused multiple failures releasing approximately 21 tons of HF, with the water mitigation system capturing most but an estimated 2-4 tons released to atmosphere. CITGO nearly exhausted its water supply during the incident, requiring emergency salt water pumping from the ship channel.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Viosca Knoll Block 900
incident__event__summary: On 22 July 2023 at approximately 1240 hours, a fire occurred on VK 900 A Platform when diesel fuel from a tote tank on the top deck spilled onto the hot turbine compressor intake housing on the production deck below. The spill occurred during a diesel transfer operation when operators left the discharge hose in the containment skid during lunch break, causing a siphoning effect that overflowed the containment. Despite firefighting efforts by 6 POB using handheld and wheel unit extinguishers, the fire spread to the 8-man sleeper and grew beyond control. The PIC ordered evacuation at approximately 1320 hours. All personnel safely evacuated via M/V Landon James with no serious injuries. Two supply vessels extinguished the fire using water cannons. Estimated damage: $1.9 million.

incident__context__region: Gulf of America, Viosca Knoll Block 915
incident__event__summary: On November 24, 2024, at approximately 1710 hours, a 3/4 inch pipe nipple on the sight glass level bridle for Compressor #1 Suction Scrubber cracked and broke, causing a gas release at VK-915A (Marlin) Tension Leg Platform. The gas alarm activated, the Control Room Operator investigated and confirmed the release, and the Emergency Shutdown System shut in the platform. A platform muster was conducted with no injuries. The compressor and scrubber were isolated, the broken nipple was replaced within 30 minutes, and production resumed. Investigation determined the nipple failed due to fatigue cracking from multiple contributing factors including possible over-tightening during installation, vibration from the compressor, and excessive weight from the sight glass level bridle. The operator will submit a Management of Change to install additional support to prevent recurrence.

incident__context__region: Gulf of America, Viosca Knoll Block 915
incident__event__summary: On 04 May 2025 at 0445 hours, the 178-foot supply vessel M/V Adriatic struck the Marlin Tension Leg Platform (VK 915 A) operated by Anadarko Petroleum Corporation. The vessel's captain was preparing paperwork for a drift test and not focused on navigation. The second captain had been given permission to leave the bridge, leaving no one actively steering the vessel. The bow contacted the Northwest pontoon and the right stern contacted the Southwest pontoon, damaging two production risers (Dorado and Crown and Anchor), a gas lift line, and firewater piping. The platform was manually ESD'd and production was shut in. No injuries or pollution occurred. Estimated damage exceeded $10 million.

incident__context__region: Gulf of America, Viosca Knoll Block 989
incident__event__summary: On December 2, 2024, at approximately 0830 hours, a gas release was discovered from subsea flowline pipeline segment 10688 at VK 989 Platform A during pressure testing of a newly replaced Subsea Control Module (SCM) pod. The gas release was detected by control room operators during testing, confirmed by ROV investigation showing gas bubbles at a 90-degree bend in the flowline. The operator immediately suspended testing and isolated the pipeline segment. The suspected cause is corrosion at a previous field repair weld that may have had imperfections. Repair options include installing an engineered clamp or replacing the damaged section with flexible pipe.

On July 3, 2023, a spinning wrench rotated approximately 30 inches and struck the Assistant Driller in the hard hat and head while he was standing at the wrench controls during drilling operations on the Enterprise 264 rig. The wrench was improperly installed with only one back-up line instead of the two required by the manufacturer's user manual, allowing excessive rotational movement. The AD was diagnosed with a concussion and evacuated to shore two days after the incident. Investigation revealed the user manual was not readily available to rig personnel, and rig personnel were trained on the job without written procedures.

On August 11, 2024, at approximately 7:10 p.m., an explosion and fires occurred on the deck of the liftboat Brazos at Vermilion Block 170 A during well cleanup and test operations. Two PTS workers were injured with second-degree burns. The incident was caused by gas escaping through improperly latched hatches on a 500-barrel atmospheric tank, creating a flammable atmosphere that was drawn into a rental diesel generator. The generator's faulty electronic control module prevented the automatic air intake shutdown device from functioning when the engine oversped, leading to ignition of the gas plume. Contributing factors included lack of written procedures, inadequate hazard analysis, poor maintenance of tank hatch O-rings, and calm wind conditions that allowed gas accumulation.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Vermilion Area Block 369
incident__event__incident_type: Uncontrolled pressure release from well tree
incident__event__summary: On April 4, 2024, at approximately 10:30 PM, a Cajun Cutters welder was injured when unexpected gas pressure was released from the top of well A-15 tree during removal of a tree cap adapter flange. The worker was impacted in the left eye and left side of face by the pressure release. The incident occurred during temporary production piping reconfiguration work for pipeline depressurization. The worker had been on duty for 15.5 hours. Lockout/Tagout procedures were not properly implemented to ensure positive isolation of all well tree valves. The injured person required medical evacuation and sustained partial permanent hearing loss and eye injuries requiring ongoing treatment.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, HI Area Block A 379
incident__event__summary: On February 21, 2024, at approximately 1349 hours, a fire occurred at the HI A 379 B platform operated by W&T Offshore during construction operations to replace corroded deck plating. While a construction fitter was grinding freshly laid welds, sparks migrated through two layers of protective fire blanket barriers into a chemical/methanol storage area approximately six feet away, igniting flammable materials. The fire was extinguished using multiple fire extinguishers and hose reels. The facility was shut down via ESD. Investigation revealed that flammable materials should have been removed per JSA requirements rather than relying on fire blanket barriers.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - West Cameron Block 33
incident__event__top_event: Wire Rope Sling Failure During Lifting Operations
incident__event__summary: On August 10, 2023, at approximately 5:15 pm, during decommissioning operations at West Cameron 33 Platform, a 2.5-ton wire rope sling parted while lifting a water abrasive cutter from a 48-inch caisson. The abrasive cutter had hung up in the caisson and was being re-lifted when it hung up again simultaneously with a wave surge. The parted sling struck an Offshore Technical Services employee on the head and neck, causing a forehead laceration requiring 16 stitches and a C6 vertebrae fracture. The incident occurred in 5-7 foot seas with the injured person standing next to the open caisson without fall protection. The JSA did not address dynamic loading from adverse sea conditions or specify the maximum lift weight relative to sling capacity.

incident__context__region: Gulf of America, West Delta Block 152
incident__event__summary: On 27 August 2025 at 1045 hours, a crane boom cable separated during a dynamic lift of an 8,000-pound Power Pack from a motor vessel to the WD-152 A platform. The crane boom fell uncontrollably, striking platform handrails and the motor vessel stern. Investigation revealed a seized bearing in the boom cable bridle sheave assembly caused the sheave pin to wear down approximately 1/2 inch. The cable cut through the Nylatron sheave material until it contacted the hardened bearing race, then cut through the sheave side, ultimately failing under slow strain. A small amount of hydraulic fluid entered the Gulf of America from broken hoses and fittings. No injuries occurred. Damage totaled $125,000.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - West Delta Block 73
incident__event__summary: On 17 February 2023 at approximately 0315 hours, a gas leak occurred on a 12-inch gas pipeline riser (PSN 759) approximately 5-7 feet above the water line at the WD 73AT production platform. The ESD was activated, shutting down the entire facility. All 26 personnel on board mustered upwind at the quarters structure and sheltered in place due to severe weather conditions (5-8 foot seas, 20-30 knot winds with gusts to 40 knots). The pipeline was bled down from 1080 psi to 0 psi. A light non-recoverable sheen (2.56 miles long, 300 feet wide) was observed during overflight. Investigation determined the leak was caused by inadequate preventative maintenance, with repairs identified in a 2015 Level 2 survey never completed, and inadequate inspection methods (drone survey on 15 Feb 2023 insufficient, no intelligent PIG inspection conducted).

incident__context__region: Gulf of America, West Delta Block 90
incident__event__summary: On April 21, 2025, at approximately 19:30hrs CDT, the West Delta 90 B platform experienced a loss of well control during Plug and Abandonment operations on well B020. While pumping fluid down the tubing to equalize pressure across a downhole plug, approximately 20 barrels of oil and gas were released from a leak at the starter head flange and 10-3/4 x 20 surface casing annulus. The uncontrolled release led to full platform evacuation. The incident resulted from a breach at the spooling head and failure within the tubing string that enabled hydraulic communication between tubing and annulus. Contributing factors included inadequate pre-job planning, failure to incorporate historical well data showing prior leakage issues, and lack of hazard assessment before continuing operations after initial leak detection.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Walker Ridge Block 51
incident__event__summary: On January 7, 2023, during drilling operations on the Transocean Deepwater Atlas drillship, a 6-5/8 inch drill pipe joint weighing 60.78 lbs/ft was dropped 58 feet onto the pipe skate when the Pipe Laydown System (PLS) failed to maintain grip. The PLS only engaged the roller jaw while the centralizer and gripper arms did not fully grip due to a hydraulic leak. The Assistant Driller could not visually confirm proper grip due to obstructed view and relied solely on the control screen which gave no indication of the partial grip or unintended movement. All personnel were in the safe step back area and no injuries occurred.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - West Delta Block 31
incident__event__summary: On 19 November 2022 at approximately 0430 hours, motor vessel Jacob Gerald allided into West Delta 31 Platform E-DRL while transporting personnel. The captain dozed off before impact. The vessel was traveling at cruising speed of 8 knots when the captain fell asleep. A production operator aboard the vessel reported back soreness and was transported to an onshore medical facility, later released to full duty (first aid case). The allision caused damage to several structural members. The platform was shut in and the damaged area barricaded. Engineering analysis determined the platform was safe to man with wave heights below 20 feet. Total repair costs remain unknown but not expected to exceed $25K.

While performing routine maintenance on a centrifugal pump, a worker removed a coupling guard. The pump was inadvertently started during troubleshooting, causing the rotating shaft to catch the worker's glove. The worker suffered a hand injury.

During crane lifting operations on an offshore deck, the load swung unexpectedly due to wind gusts. A rigger attempted to steady the load manually and suffered a shoulder injury. Pre-job briefing did not address wind limits and spotter coverage was insufficient.

incident__event__top_event: Worker entered confined space without permit
incident__event__summary: A worker entered a confined space to inspect a vessel without completing the permit. Gas testing was not performed and ventilation was not set up. The worker experienced dizziness and required medical evaluation.
A pressurized hose ruptured during line flushing, striking a technician and causing lacerations.
A gas leak occurred at the north valve station due to severe corrosion on the main flange. The leak was detected by the Fire & Gas Detection system, triggering an automatic ESD System shutdown.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, East Breaks Block 699
incident__event__summary: On November 16, 2022, at 05:11 AM, approximately twelve barrels of Synthetic Based Mud (SBM) were discharged into the Gulf of Mexico from the Diamond Offshore drillship West Vela during exploration drilling operations at 25,000 feet depth. A saltwater kick of fifty-eight barrels occurred below the base of salt, causing the Managed Pressure Drilling (MPD) system Surface Back Pressure to exceed 1,300 psi, triggering Pressure Relief Valves that were lined up to discharge overboard instead of to the flowline. The well was successfully shut in with shut-in drill pipe pressure of 1,600 psi, and the kick was circulated out. The MPD system was not designed to control influxes greater than five barrels.

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Control of PIG Launcher Cap

incident__event__summary: During 5-year recertification of forward starboard crane on Noble Stanley Lafosse, winch brake assembly failure occurred at 70 MT during load test to 93.5 MT. Crane was overloaded due to AOPS activation over sealift area. Four brake assemblies didn't release simultaneously, causing sprag clutch failure and uncontrolled descent of water bag. Hydraulic test port failure resulted in LOPC of hydraulic fluid onto rig deck.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Green Canyon Block 432
incident__event__summary: On November 10, 2024, an injured person (IP) was struck by a wave on the stern of the Transocean Drillship Deepwater Invictus during workover operations. Despite being informed that the stern was secured due to deteriorating weather conditions, the IP went to the aft handrail to observe the water. A 15-foot wave struck the IP, throwing him approximately 20 feet and causing a fractured right femur. The IP was evacuated to hospital for treatment.

A rope access technician experienced an uncontrolled descent of 90 feet while inspecting a corroded Seawater Reject Caisson, resulting in serious leg injuries. The fall occurred due to improper threading of safety equipment and failure of backup systems.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Alaminos Canyon Block 857
incident__event__summary: On June 21, 2023, at 0930 hours, a Variable Frequency Device A (VFD-A) failed at Shell's Perdido Spar platform. The B-phase reactor of the VFD-A failed to ground, releasing smoke that activated smoke detectors in the Subsea Motor Control Center room. This triggered an automated facility shut-in and personnel muster. No injuries or pollution occurred, but the VFD-A sustained fire damage requiring $65,000 replacement cost.

On June 9, 2009, a catastrophic natural gas explosion occurred at the ConAgra Slim Jim production facility during installation and commissioning of a new water heater. The explosion was caused by purging natural gas into an occupied utility room over 2.5 hours without proper ventilation or gas detection. The incident resulted in 4 deaths, multiple injuries including 67 hospitalizations, structural damage, and secondary ammonia release of 18,000 pounds.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Green Canyon Block 432
Gulf of Mexico - Alaminos Canyon Block 857

incident__event__summary: A lifting incident occurred due to a crane brake failure during a load test, resulting in a loss of primary containment and hydraulic fluid spill.

incident__event__top_event: Injury during slip and cut drill line operations
incident__event__summary: A Seadrill employee sustained a back and shoulder injury during slip and cut drill line operations when a drill line fell after slipping from a nylon web sling.
A lifting incident occurred on the jack-up, Enterprise 205, resulting in damage to the crown saver assembly due to an overpull while pulling out of the hole.
An injured person was struck by a wave while observing the water from a secured area, resulting in a fractured right femur.
Two individuals were evacuated due to injuries sustained during a routine life capsule inspection. One individual suffered a serious head injury.
A crane stinger failed during the offloading of tools, causing a basket to drop and damage the control room and platform.
During crane operations, the deck foreman was struck by a load of casing due to turning his back to the suspended load.
On January 18, 2024, the S-90/MT connection of 22" casing failed, resulting in 72 joints falling partially into the hole and on the seafloor.
On February 18, 2023, a stand of drill pipe was dropped on the rig floor, injuring one Floor Hand.

incident__event__top_event: Amputation of fingers during marine growth removal
incident__event__summary: An injury occurred during decommissioning operations when the injured person attempted to adjust chains in a marine growth removal system, leading to the amputation of two fingers.
An injury requiring evacuation occurred when a rigger's hand was caught in a crane block during abandonment operations.
A fire occurred in the Glycol Dehydration system due to a plugged still column vent pipe, resulting in significant damage.
A 25 pound riser bolt insert dislodged and fell approximately 75 feet, damaging two steps of a portable aluminum stairway.

A hand injury requiring evacuation occurred on the drillship Noble Stanley Lafosse when the injured person (IP) was assisting in disconnecting wireline tools, resulting in lacerations and a broken finger.
An incident occurred when a Transocean Subsea Superintendent fell through an open hole on a work platform during BOP maintenance, resulting in injuries.

incident__event__top_event: Uncontrolled descent of Rope Access Technician
incident__event__summary: A Rope Access Technician experienced an uncontrolled descent of 90 feet while inspecting the Seawater Reject Caisson, resulting in significant injuries.
A lifting incident occurred due to a stinger being latched to the crane boom, leading to structural damage.
A Derrickman was injured while attempting to secure a malfunctioning fingerboard latch on the upper fingerboards of the Pipe Racking System.
A deckhand on the M/V Harvey Freedom support vessel injured his leg while attempting to steady a load during back-loading operations.

On June 9, 2009, a catastrophic natural gas explosion at the ConAgra Slim Jim production facility resulted in four deaths, multiple injuries, and significant structural damage. The explosion was linked to improper gas line purging practices during the installation of a new water heater.
The CSB implements guidelines to ensure quality and integrity in information dissemination.
An explosion occurred at the TS USA facility due to water trapped in a hollow part, leading to a molten salt eruption and one fatality.
The CSB recommended improvements in managing reactive hazards after several high-consequence incidents.

incident__event__top_event: Explosion and Fire due to Dead Leg Rupture
incident__event__summary: A dead leg ruptured, releasing butadiene and causing explosions and fires, resulting in injuries and significant property damage.

incident__event__top_event: Explosion of nitrous oxide trailer truck
incident__event__summary: A nitrous oxide trailer truck exploded at the Airgas manufacturing facility, killing one employee and halting production indefinitely.
An explosion occurred in the Electrostatic Precipitator at the ExxonMobil Torrance refinery due to pressure deviation allowing hydrocarbons to backflow and ignite.
A vapor cloud ignited at the BP-Husky refinery due to the intentional release of flammable liquid naphtha, resulting in two fatalities and significant damage.
An estimated 200,000 gallons of gasoline overflowed from a storage tank, leading to a large vapor cloud that ignited, causing explosions and fire.

incident__event__top_event: Explosion and Fire at Gas Plant
incident__event__summary: A major loss of containment at the Enterprise Products Pascagoula Gas Plant resulted in the release of hydrocarbons, leading to fires and explosions.

incident__event__top_event: Tank fire and loss of containment
incident__event__summary: A loss of containment and fire occurred at the ITC terminal facility, resulting in the destruction of fifteen tanks and environmental contamination.

incident__event__top_event: Fatal chemical release due to inadvertent removal of valve components
incident__event__summary: During maintenance, workers inadvertently removed pressure-retaining components from a plug valve, leading to a release of acetic acid that fatally injured two workers and seriously injured another.
A blowout occurred at the Macondo oil well, leading to explosions, fire, and multiple fatalities.

incident__event__top_event: fire eruption due to tube rupture
incident__event__summary: A fire erupted when a metal tube within a fired heater ruptured during the initial startup of a renewable diesel hydroprocessing unit, leading to significant injuries and damages.
An explosion occurred at the Motiva refinery due to a spark igniting flammable vapors from a corroded tank, resulting in one death and significant environmental damage.
A blowout and rig fire occurred at Pryor Trust 0718 gas well number 1H-9, resulting in the fatalities of five workers due to thermal burns and smoke inhalation.
The CSB recommends developing or revising publications for remote isolation devices in various facilities.
The incident involved a loss of well control during a workover operation, leading to a blowout and a flash fire that resulted in fatalities and injuries.

incident__event__top_event: Explosion and fire at Yenkin-Majestic Paint Corporation
incident__event__summary: A flammable liquid mixture and its vapors became pressurized and released through the seal of a closed manway, forming a flammable cloud that ignited and exploded, resulting in a fire and multiple injuries.
An explosion occurred during the planned cleaning of new piping at Kleen Energy, resulting in six fatalities and injuries to at least 50 personnel.
A worker suffered a hand injury when a centrifugal pump was inadvertently started during troubleshooting.
A rigger suffered a shoulder injury while attempting to steady a load that swung unexpectedly due to wind gusts during crane lifting operations.
A worker entered a confined space to inspect a vessel without completing the permit, leading to dizziness and a need for medical evaluation.
A pressurized hose ruptured during line flushing, striking a technician and causing lacerations.
A worker suffered a hand injury when a centrifugal pump was inadvertently started during troubleshooting.

incident__event__top_event: load swung unexpectedly during crane lifting operations
incident__event__summary: A rigger suffered a shoulder injury while attempting to steady a load that swung unexpectedly due to wind gusts.

incident__event__top_event: worker entered confined space without permit
incident__event__summary: A worker entered a confined space to inspect a vessel without completing the permit. Gas testing was not performed and ventilation was not set up. The worker experienced dizziness and required medical evaluation.

incident__event__incident_type: Crane brake failure during load testing with hydraulic fluid release
incident__event__summary: On May 2, 2024, during 5-year recertification load testing of forward starboard knuckle boom crane (KBC #1) on the drillship Noble Stanley Lafosse at GC 478, a winch brake assembly failure occurred at approximately 70 MT while attempting to reach 93.5 MT test load. The crane's Automatic Overload Protection System (AOPS) activated, but brake assembly #4 failed to release simultaneously with the other three brakes. This caused excessive load on the sprag clutches which sheared, resulting in uncontrolled descent of the water bag. The descent caused a hydraulic test port failure, spraying hydraulic fluid on deck (LOPC). The water bag contacted the take-on station, ripped open, and descended to the Gulf of Mexico. Two hydraulic motors and four brake assemblies were damaged. Total property damage was $168,150. No injuries occurred.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Keathley Canyon Block 785
incident__event__incident_type: Personnel Injury - Slip/Trip/Fall with Struck By
incident__event__summary: On 7 March 2024 at 1500 hours, a Seadrill employee sustained a back and shoulder injury during slip and cut drill line operations on the Seadrill West Neptune drillship. While attempting to correct an abnormal twist in a 2-inch diameter drill line using an air tugger and nylon web sling, the drill line slipped from the sling and fell approximately 20-25 feet. The 19-foot section weighing 148 pounds struck the employee who had tripped while exiting the line of fire, hitting his hardhat, rolling over his middle back and left shoulder. He was diagnosed with a torn labrum and required 10-12 weeks of physical therapy.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - South Timbalier Block 23
incident__event__summary: On July 8, 2024, while pulling out of the hole with drill pipe on the jack-up rig Enterprise 205, the traveling block struck the crown saver assembly with approximately 117,000 lbs overpull, resulting in $11,210 in damages. The driller lost situational awareness while checking the pipe tally, and the crown-o-matic (COM) failed to trip when contacted by the drill line because the barrel wasn't set properly. Contributing factors included inadequate hazard analysis in the JSA, lack of rig-specific procedures for checking the COM, and failure of floorhand to count tool joints during the operation.

incident__context__region: Gulf of America, Green Canyon Block 432
incident__event__summary: On November 10, 2024, at 0145 hours, an injured person (IP) was struck by a wave on the stern of the Transocean Deepwater Invictus drillship during workover operations. Despite being informed during a pre-tour meeting that the stern was secured due to deteriorating weather and building seas (approximately 15-foot waves), the IP entered the restricted area to observe the water. A wave struck the IP, throwing him approximately 20 feet across the rig, causing him to strike a bollard before coming to rest at the base of a stairwell. The IP sustained a fractured right femur and was evacuated to West Jefferson Hospital. The investigation concluded that the IP's failure to observe the secured area and follow supervisor direction contributed significantly to the accident.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - High Island Area Block A-5
incident__event__summary: On July 11, 2024, at approximately 0730 hours, during a routine life capsule inspection at the High Island A-5 Manta Ray Gathering Facility, a contract pipeline operator from Quality Process Services suffered a serious head injury when struck by a winch assist handle. The Genesis Person in Charge and the injured person lowered the capsule 4 to 6 feet to verify winch operations. After lowering, the PIC engaged the electric winch from the lower section to raise the capsule. The winch assist handle, which should have been removed before operating the electric winch, remained in the socket. As the winch operated, the handle struck the IP in the head, causing him to fall unconscious with head bleeding. A safety device designed to prevent winch operation with the handle in place had been bypassed. The IP was diagnosed with a fractured skull requiring surgery. The incident was attributed to inadequate knowledge of equipment operation and failure to follow safety protocols, with contributing factors including lack of Job Safety Analysis and improper equipment handling.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Ship Shoal Block 189
incident__event__summary: On March 13, 2024, during plug and abandon operations on jack-up rig Enterprise 264, a crane stinger failed while offloading a 4x40 foot basket weighing 13,000 pounds from a workboat. The stinger, rated for 9.80 tons vertical pull, parted due to internal corrosion from moisture buildup in the socket over time. The basket fell upside down onto the control room, P-tanks, and platform on top of living quarters, causing approximately $7,500 in damage. No injuries occurred. Investigation revealed that the stinger had not been specifically inspected prior to use, and inspection procedures (Preventive Maintenance Daily checklist, cold work permit, and JSA) did not explicitly require stinger inspection.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Walker Ridge Block 29
incident__event__summary: On February 16, 2024, during crane operations offloading 16-inch casing from a supply vessel to the pipe deck on the Nabors MODS 400 rig, a deck foreman (IP) was struck by a swinging bundle of casing weighing approximately 11,500 lbs. The IP had turned his back to the suspended load while verifying casing tally and failed to recognize he was positioned in the impact zone between the casing bundle and a sub-rack basket. Despite warnings from riggers, the bundle struck the IP on the right hip, forcing his left hip into the sub-rack basket, resulting in three pelvic fractures. The IP was evacuated to hospital and discharged four days later.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Green Canyon Block 478
incident__event__summary: On January 18, 2024, while running 22 inch casing on the drillship Noble Stanley Lafosse at GC 478 Well 004, the S-90/MT connection between joints 16 and 17 failed due to cross-threading. When the slips were opened to install anti-rotation keys, 72 joints of casing fell partially into the hole and onto the seafloor, impacting subsea infrastructure. The connection was made up only 1/4 to 3/8 turn instead of the required 1/2 to 3/4 turn, and visual inspection failed to detect a 0.143 inch gap indicating cross-threading. No injuries or environmental impacts occurred, but damages exceeded $15 million.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Mississippi Canyon Block 778
incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment of Suspended Load
incident__event__summary: On February 18, 2023, at 21:27 hours, while pulling out of the hole with the 13th stand of 4-1/2 inch drill pipe on the Thunder Horse PDQ Platform, the Driller opened the BX4 elevator before the Pipe Racking System (PRS) could secure the stand. This caused the stand of drill pipe to fall across the rig floor towards the port side, landing on the auxiliary PRS hoist. Three Floor Hands in the yellow zone evacuated the area, with one slipping on the deck and injuring his knee and hip. The Floor Hand was evacuated the next day for medical evaluation. The incident could have resulted in multiple fatalities. Investigation revealed the Driller had a similar incident on January 3, 2023, and was not following Work Instructions WI-TH-DR-OPS-096.

incident__context__region: Pacific OCS Region - Lease P00205, Area/Block LA/6012
incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment of Human Body Part
incident__event__summary: On June 18, 2023, at approximately 18:35, an amputation injury occurred during decommissioning operations at Platform Gail. The injured person was adjusting chains in a marine growth removal system when a conductor began rising unexpectedly. The injured person had requested an 'all stop' and seeing movement stop, placed his left hand fingers under the chain just as the conductor began rising again. This motion caused the chain to tighten, trapping the injured person's left hand and amputating the tips of two fingers (middle and ring finger between fingernail and first knuckle). The incident occurred because the 'all stop' call was not confirmed by the casing jack operator, who was simultaneously checking the conductor's weight. The injured person mistook the brief pause for a confirmed stop and proceeded with chain adjustment.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Ship Shoal Area Block 224
incident__event__incident_type: Personnel Injury - Hand Caught in Crane Equipment
incident__event__summary: On July 20, 2024, at approximately 2200 hours, a rigger suffered lacerations to his ring finger and partial amputation of his pinky finger when his right hand was caught between the crane cable and crane block sheave while guiding a casing cutter tool through deck openings during abandonment operations at Ship Shoal 224 D platform. The injured person was using his hands to guide the crane block instead of push poles as required by company policy. The incident occurred during a blind lift operation where the crane operator could not see the load due to the lift boat being jacked up above the platform deck level.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - High Island Area Block 547
incident__event__summary: On January 5, 2023, at 1730 hours, a fire occurred at High Island 547 B platform involving the Glycol Reboiler Dehydration system. The still column vent pipe became plugged, resulting in pressure build up until it ruptured. The rupture caused glycol to spray which contacted the reboiler and exhaust stack (operating at 750-800°F), igniting the glycol mixture. Platform operators activated emergency shutdown and extinguished the fire in ten minutes using fire extinguishers. No injuries or pollution occurred. Fire damage was limited to the Glycol Reboiler and Dehydration unit, totaling over $100,000.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Alaminos Canyon Block 857
incident__event__summary: On January 3, 2023, at 2210 hours, a DC capacitor on Universal Power Supply UPS-7560 failed at the Perdido Spar platform, causing smoke release, arcing/sparking, and activation of smoke detectors. The automated safety system shut-in the facility and caused full power loss. The Fire Team responded but found no visible fire. The Electrical Team isolated power feeds and identified the failed inverter through troubleshooting. The UPS was locked out and bypassed, with a spare circuit providing power. Estimated repair cost is $56,830 with 7-9 weeks lead time for parts. No injuries or pollution occurred.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Walker Ridge Block 758
incident__event__summary: On March 21, 2023, at 02:30 hours, a 25-pound riser bolt insert dislodged from a riser flange and fell approximately 75 feet to the rig floor on the drillship Valaris DS-18. The insert passed between two Floorhands and struck an aluminum stairway, damaging two steps. The drill crew was running marine riser joint #36 using the Top Drive when the incident occurred. Investigation revealed that the riser retainer pin was either never installed or fell out during transport, and the required retention cable (sling) was not installed as specified in Step 6, Item 'd' of the WIMS. Personnel failed to stop work immediately after the incident and continued making up riser bolts for several minutes before reporting.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Green Canyon Block 389
incident__event__summary: On April 22, 2024, a hand injury occurred on the drillship Noble Stanley Lafosse during wireline completion operations at Green Canyon Block 389. The injured person (IP) was assisting the wireline helper disconnect wireline tools when his right hand was caught between the upper C-plate with tools weighing 175 lbs and lower C-plate. The helper had raised the tool string approximately 4 inches above the bottom C-plate. The IP placed his right hand under the upper C-plate to stabilize the tool string. When the helper released the tools, they fell 4 inches, smashing the IP's right hand between the C-plates, causing lacerations to the right hand ring and middle fingertips and a broken finger tip. The IP required evacuation and 14 stitches. Investigation revealed the helper lacked deepwater experience, the IP was not properly trained on wireline tools, the JSEA was inadequate, communication was poor, and Stop Work Authority was not used.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Walker Ridge Block 51
incident__event__summary: On March 28, 2024, the Transocean Subsea Superintendent fell approximately nine feet through an open hole on a BOP work platform on the drillship Transocean Deepwater Atlas. The hatch cover had been removed after its hinge separated, creating a dropped object hazard, but no barrier was installed and the open hole was not communicated to the work team. The IP was wearing a safety harness but was not tied off. He injured his knee and shin and was evacuated for medical treatment. The remote control box he was carrying fell an additional 40 feet. Investigation found failures in communication, hazard identification, and implementation of corrective actions.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Green Canyon Block 52
incident__event__summary: On May 31, 2024, during boat offloading/backloading operations at Green Canyon Block 52 Platform A, a stinger attached to the crane's main block latched onto a lace of the crane boom. When the PIC instructed the crane operator to lower the boom without simultaneously lowering the main block (ignoring CO-2's warning), the stinger pulled free and broke a weld on the boom lace. Despite discovering the damage the next day during pre-use inspection, the PIC instructed crew to continue using the damaged crane. The crane remained in operation from May 31 until BSEE inspectors took it out of service on June 6, 2024. BSEE was not immediately notified of the incident as required.

incident__context__region: Gulf of America - Alaminos Canyon Block 337
incident__event__incident_type: Caught Between - Personnel Pinned by Equipment
incident__event__summary: On October 12, 2024, a Derrickman (IP) was injured onboard the Noble Valiant drilling rig during tripping operations. The IP positioned himself on the upper fingerboards, outside the designated safe observation area, without a working-at-heights permit to tie up a malfunctioning fingerboard latch. While working on the latch for approximately 10 minutes, the Pipe Racking System (PRS), operating in automatic mode, extended to retrieve a stand of pipe. The IP was caught and pinned between the PRS arm and the pipe stand. Coworkers heard his calls for help, the AD engaged the emergency stop, and the IP was freed and evacuated for medical treatment. The incident resulted from policy violations and lack of situational awareness by multiple parties.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Alaminos Canyon Block 337
incident__event__summary: On 27 September 2024, a Deckhand on the M/V Harvey Freedom support vessel injured his leg during equipment back-loading operations from the Noble Valiant drillship. While attempting to steady a 50-foot basket with a tagline as it was being landed, the support vessel rolled causing the IP to lose footing and injure his left leg. The IP was evacuated to the drillship, then to shore, required surgery, and resulted in more than 3-days lost time.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Alaminos Canyon Block 735
incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Personnel in Line of Fire
incident__event__summary: On December 8, 2023, at approximately 08:30 am, a Floorhand (IP) sustained a lost time injury when contacted by the Top Drive System (TDS) while positioned on the port pipe racker upper access platform approximately 80 feet above the drill floor on the Noble Globetrotter II drilling rig. The IP had moved to the platform to troubleshoot a 'slack rope detected' alarm without notifying the Driller. As the Driller lowered the TDS during tripping operations, the lower port TDS roller assembly contacted the IP, folding his upper torso around the outside handrail. The IP sustained a punctured lung, fractured ribs, and a fractured scapula. The incident resulted from inadequate communication, failure to identify the moving equipment hazard in the JSA, lack of warning signage, and absence of engineered barriers despite only 3 inches clearance between the TDS and platform handrail.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Alaminos Canyon Block 857
incident__event__summary: On October 3, 2024, at approximately 9:00 am, an arc flash fire occurred on Shell's Perdido SPAR when a Silicon Controlled Rectifier (SCR) failed in the SCR house. During wireline logging operations, the cement pump was being used to pump base oil into well GA017 for hydrate mitigation when the cement unit's electronic panel lost power. When the Tool Pusher and Rig Electrician entered the SCR room to investigate, they heard a loud popping sound and observed smoke. Evidence of arcing between DC buss bars, arcing on circuit breaker heat sinks, and a failed buss insulator in SCR cabinet #1 was identified. The fire and gas alarm system did not detect the fire; it was only identified when personnel entered the SCR house. The well was secured and SCR cabinet #1 was isolated for repair.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Alaminos Canyon Block 857
incident__event__incident_type: Unauthorized discharge of drilling fluid to sea
incident__event__summary: On September 4, 2023, approximately 24 barrels of Synthetic Based Mud (SBM) were discharged into the Gulf of Mexico from the H&P 205 drilling rig on Shell's Perdido Spar. The crew was circulating a clean out run and had transferred 117 bbls of SBM to processing pits to remove entrained gas. Seven minutes after the transfer pump was turned off, the degasser tank volume began decreasing. Investigation revealed that both the knife valve on the degasser tank discharge line and the butterfly valve (master dump valve) had leaked due to obstructions - cuttings in the knife valve and a piece of rubber in the butterfly valve - preventing proper sealing. The crew had visually verified valve positions per procedure but did not functionally test the valves prior to transfer. The valves had not been operated since April 2023.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Alaminos Canyon Block 857
incident__event__summary: On June 21, 2023, at 0930 hours, a Variable Frequency Device A (VFD-A) failed at the Perdido Spar platform. The B-phase reactor of the VFD-A failed to ground, releasing smoke that activated smoke detectors in the Subsea Motor Control Center room. This triggered an automated facility shut-in and personnel muster. The Fire Team responded but found no active fire, only smoke. Cabinet A reached 350°F and was isolated from service. Estimated damage cost was $65,000. No injuries or pollution occurred.

On June 9, 2009, a catastrophic natural gas explosion occurred at the ConAgra Slim Jim production facility in Garner, North Carolina, resulting in 4 deaths, 3 critical burn injuries, 1 amputation, and 67 total hospitalizations. The explosion occurred during installation and commissioning of a new 5-million BTU per hour gas-fired industrial water heater. Over a two-and-a-half hour period, natural gas was purged from new piping into an interior utility room rather than vented outdoors. Personnel relied on sense of smell rather than combustible gas detectors to monitor gas concentration. The gas accumulated to explosive levels and ignited, causing serious structural damage including 37% roof collapse. The explosion also damaged ammonia refrigeration system piping, releasing approximately 18,000 pounds of toxic anhydrous ammonia to the environment, contaminating local surface waters and causing a fish kill. Over 200 nonessential personnel were present in the facility during the purging activity.

incident__context__region: Gulf of America, East Breaks Block 602
incident__event__summary: On 21-May-2025 at approximately 1:45 p.m., a crane incident occurred on the Nansen Spar platform. Contract scaffold builders attached multiple 1/4-ton lever hoists to the crane boom for scaffolding stabilization without notifying the Lifting Authority. When the crane operator lifted the boom out of its rest after lunch break without conducting a pre-use inspection, one lever hoist broke and fell approximately 30 feet to the flare boom mezzanine deck. No injuries occurred. The incident resulted from communication failures, inadequate supervision, and simultaneous operations in a confined area.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - East Cameron Block 71
incident__event__summary: On May 25, 2023, at 1:15am, approximately 79 barrels of zinc bromide brine was inadvertently discharged from a wellhead casing valve at Well Number C007 on Platform C at East Cameron Block 71. During BOP testing operations, a Floorhand was assigned to close a 1 13/16-inch 10M gate valve on the wellhead casing. The Floorhand believed the valve was closed but it was not fully closed. When zinc bromide brine was pumped to refill the production casing and BOP, the partially open valve allowed fluid to discharge into the Gulf of Mexico. The Floorhand noticed slower than normal returns and was sent to recheck the valve, where he fully closed it and stopped the flow. Investigation using Pit Volume Totalizer data determined the actual release was 79 barrels, significantly more than the initially reported 28 barrels.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Eugene Island Block 158
incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment of Heavy Equipment
incident__event__summary: On 21 September 2023 at approximately 0130 hours, a night shift Supervisor sustained a compound fracture with exposed bone on his lower left shin when an unsecured 8000-pound wellhead rolled onto his leg after he sat on it and stood up. The wellhead had been removed from Well #13 during day shift operations and laid on its side on the deck of the Lift Boat Man-O-War without proper securing. Personnel had attempted to check stability by hand before removing rigging, but this was insufficient. The wellhead was in an unstable position and may have shifted due to lift boat movements from weather and crane activity. The injured party was evacuated by medevac helicopter for medical treatment, resulting in days away from work.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Eugene Island Block 252
incident__event__top_event: Structural Damage - Crane Auxiliary Jib Detachment
incident__event__summary: On August 20, 2023, at approximately 1000 hours, the auxiliary jib on the platform crane detached while attempting to relocate a construction supply box weighing 3,886 lbs. The crane operator failed to center the auxiliary line over the construction supply box, causing it to become lodged on a capping beam. Continued lifting created a side loading effect that caused the boom to twist, shearing the auxiliary jib bolts and causing the jib to detach. The auxiliary jib became entangled in hydraulic lines, preventing it from falling to the deck. The rigger failed to communicate the all stop signal. No injuries occurred. Repairs totaled $80,000.

incident__context__region: Gulf of America - Eugene Island Block 259
incident__event__summary: On October 3, 2025, at approximately 1230 hours, a contract fitter sustained a severe injury to his right leg during decommissioning operations on the White Fleet Operating OCS-G00985 Eugene Island 259 C Facility. While attempting to install stabilizing brackets to support piping during platform removal operations, a section of 8-inch pipe was cut approximately 20 feet away from the CF. Once the section was cut, the remaining pipe shifted and slid towards the CF, pinning his right leg between a flange on the cut pipe and the piping the CF's leg was draped across. The CF suffered multiple fractures of the right femur. The incident was caused by inadequate job instructions and failure to communicate crew locations, and inadequate hazard analysis that failed to address potential pipe movement due to stored energy.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Eugene Island Block 259
incident__event__summary: On October 13, 2023, at approximately 1745 hours, a crane mechanic fell approximately 96 ft from the gantry of a crane into the water at Cox Operating's Eugene Island 259 C Facility. While attempting to replace the auxiliary winch, the crane mechanic positioned himself over the handrail without wearing a safety harness or life vest to remove a brace blocking the winch installation. When the second bolt broke, the brace detached and contacted the crane mechanic, causing him to fall over the handrails. He suffered a fractured rib, dislocated shoulder, and internal bleeding.

incident__context__region: Gulf of America, Eugene Island Block 307, Area EI
incident__event__summary: On 17 March 2025 at 2140 hours, during well abandonment operations at Eugene Island Block 307A, a single joint of 3.5-inch pipe fell approximately 40 feet to the deck. The incident occurred when a crane operator, without clear line of sight, lowered the pipe onto a Self-Retracting Lifeline (SRL) Arm in an elevated hydraulic jack basket. When the floor hand pushed the pipe off the SRL arm, the impact caused worn elevator doors to open, releasing the pipe. Two workers in the area moved out of the way before the pipe struck a hydraulic hose and fire extinguisher handle. No injuries or pollution occurred; damages totaled $4,000.

incident__context__region: Gulf of America - Garden Banks Block 215
incident__event__summary: On 17 July 2025 at 0752 hours, during workover operations at Garden Banks Block 215, a 0.375-inch high-pressure hose ruptured while blowing down a coil tubing reel with nitrogen. The ruptured hose struck a Noble Assistant Driller on his left arm and an Oil States employee on the left side of his face. A Schlumberger Operator injured his knee while moving to avoid being struck. All three injuries were classified as First Aid Only. Laboratory analysis determined the hose failed due to chlorides corrosion coupled with rapid oxidation of wire reinforcement strands. The 3000 psi rated hose had only been in use for approximately 6 months and was pressurized to 2400 psi when it ruptured. Investigation found no safety restraints were installed on the manifold as recommended in Oil States Standard Operating Procedure, and there was no established hose inspection procedure or preventative maintenance program in place.

incident__context__region: Gulf of America, Mississippi Canyon Block 773
incident__event__summary: On 16 July 2025 at 1810 hours, an external walkway platform on the third floor of a Temporary Living Quarters (TLQ) at Devils Tower SPAR platform unexpectedly detached from the TLQ structure by 12-18 inches while three Nabors employees were standing on it. The walkway remained held by stanchions and stairway connections. Investigation revealed inadequate welded tabs that lacked sufficient surface area for long-term support, combined with 23 years of marine corrosion and fatigue. The TLQ was originally designed as temporary housing in 2002 but remained in service. Visual-only inspections failed to detect weld deterioration. No injuries or dropped objects occurred due to immediate securing actions.

incident__context__region: Gulf of America, Vermilion Block 38, Lease 00204
incident__event__summary: On July 12, 2025, at approximately 3:30 p.m., a casing lifting pin failed during well abandonment operations on the VR-38M platform.
