The fire caused multiple failures releasing approximately 21 tons of HF, with the water mitigation system capturing most but an estimated 2-4 tons released to atmosphere. CITGO nearly exhausted its water supply during the incident, requiring emergency salt water pumping from the ship channel.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Viosca Knoll Block 900
incident__event__summary: On 22 July 2023 at approximately 1240 hours, a fire occurred on VK 900 A Platform when diesel fuel from a tote tank on the top deck spilled onto the hot turbine compressor intake housing on the production deck below. The spill occurred during a diesel transfer operation when operators left the discharge hose in the containment skid during lunch break, causing a siphoning effect that overflowed the containment. Despite firefighting efforts by 6 POB using handheld and wheel unit extinguishers, the fire spread to the 8-man sleeper and grew beyond control. The PIC ordered evacuation at approximately 1320 hours. All personnel safely evacuated via M/V Landon James with no serious injuries. Two supply vessels extinguished the fire using water cannons. Estimated damage: $1.9 million.

incident__context__region: Gulf of America, Viosca Knoll Block 915
incident__event__summary: On November 24, 2024, at approximately 1710 hours, a 3/4 inch pipe nipple on the sight glass level bridle for Compressor #1 Suction Scrubber cracked and broke, causing a gas release at VK-915A (Marlin) Tension Leg Platform. The gas alarm activated, the Control Room Operator investigated and confirmed the release, and the Emergency Shutdown System shut in the platform. A platform muster was conducted with no injuries. The compressor and scrubber were isolated, the broken nipple was replaced within 30 minutes, and production resumed. Investigation determined the nipple failed due to fatigue cracking from multiple contributing factors including possible over-tightening during installation, vibration from the compressor, and excessive weight from the sight glass level bridle. The operator will submit a Management of Change to install additional support to prevent recurrence.

incident__context__region: Gulf of America, Viosca Knoll Block 915
incident__event__summary: On 04 May 2025 at 0445 hours, the 178-foot supply vessel M/V Adriatic struck the Marlin Tension Leg Platform (VK 915 A) operated by Anadarko Petroleum Corporation. The vessel's captain was preparing paperwork for a drift test and not focused on navigation. The second captain had been given permission to leave the bridge, leaving no one actively steering the vessel. The bow contacted the Northwest pontoon and the right stern contacted the Southwest pontoon, damaging two production risers (Dorado and Crown and Anchor), a gas lift line, and firewater piping. The platform was manually ESD'd and production was shut in. No injuries or pollution occurred. Estimated damage exceeded $10 million.

incident__context__region: Gulf of America, Viosca Knoll Block 989
incident__event__summary: On December 2, 2024, at approximately 0830 hours, a gas release was discovered from subsea flowline pipeline segment 10688 at VK 989 Platform A during pressure testing of a newly replaced Subsea Control Module (SCM) pod. The gas release was detected by control room operators during testing, confirmed by ROV investigation showing gas bubbles at a 90-degree bend in the flowline. The operator immediately suspended testing and isolated the pipeline segment. The suspected cause is corrosion at a previous field repair weld that may have had imperfections. Repair options include installing an engineered clamp or replacing the damaged section with flexible pipe.

On July 3, 2023, a spinning wrench rotated approximately 30 inches and struck the Assistant Driller in the hard hat and head while he was standing at the wrench controls during drilling operations on the Enterprise 264 rig. The wrench was improperly installed with only one back-up line instead of the two required by the manufacturer's user manual, allowing excessive rotational movement. The AD was diagnosed with a concussion and evacuated to shore two days after the incident. Investigation revealed the user manual was not readily available to rig personnel, and rig personnel were trained on the job without written procedures.

On August 11, 2024, at approximately 7:10 p.m., an explosion and fires occurred on the deck of the liftboat Brazos at Vermilion Block 170 A during well cleanup and test operations. Two PTS workers were injured with second-degree burns. The incident was caused by gas escaping through improperly latched hatches on a 500-barrel atmospheric tank, creating a flammable atmosphere that was drawn into a rental diesel generator. The generator's faulty electronic control module prevented the automatic air intake shutdown device from functioning when the engine oversped, leading to ignition of the gas plume. Contributing factors included lack of written procedures, inadequate hazard analysis, poor maintenance of tank hatch O-rings, and calm wind conditions that allowed gas accumulation.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Vermilion Area Block 369
incident__event__incident_type: Uncontrolled pressure release from well tree
incident__event__summary: On April 4, 2024, at approximately 10:30 PM, a Cajun Cutters welder was injured when unexpected gas pressure was released from the top of well A-15 tree during removal of a tree cap adapter flange. The worker was impacted in the left eye and left side of face by the pressure release. The incident occurred during temporary production piping reconfiguration work for pipeline depressurization. The worker had been on duty for 15.5 hours. Lockout/Tagout procedures were not properly implemented to ensure positive isolation of all well tree valves. The injured person required medical evacuation and sustained partial permanent hearing loss and eye injuries requiring ongoing treatment.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, HI Area Block A 379
incident__event__summary: On February 21, 2024, at approximately 1349 hours, a fire occurred at the HI A 379 B platform operated by W&T Offshore during construction operations to replace corroded deck plating. While a construction fitter was grinding freshly laid welds, sparks migrated through two layers of protective fire blanket barriers into a chemical/methanol storage area approximately six feet away, igniting flammable materials. The fire was extinguished using multiple fire extinguishers and hose reels. The facility was shut down via ESD. Investigation revealed that flammable materials should have been removed per JSA requirements rather than relying on fire blanket barriers.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - West Cameron Block 33
incident__event__top_event: Wire Rope Sling Failure During Lifting Operations
incident__event__summary: On August 10, 2023, at approximately 5:15 pm, during decommissioning operations at West Cameron 33 Platform, a 2.5-ton wire rope sling parted while lifting a water abrasive cutter from a 48-inch caisson. The abrasive cutter had hung up in the caisson and was being re-lifted when it hung up again simultaneously with a wave surge. The parted sling struck an Offshore Technical Services employee on the head and neck, causing a forehead laceration requiring 16 stitches and a C6 vertebrae fracture. The incident occurred in 5-7 foot seas with the injured person standing next to the open caisson without fall protection. The JSA did not address dynamic loading from adverse sea conditions or specify the maximum lift weight relative to sling capacity.

incident__context__region: Gulf of America, West Delta Block 152
incident__event__summary: On 27 August 2025 at 1045 hours, a crane boom cable separated during a dynamic lift of an 8,000-pound Power Pack from a motor vessel to the WD-152 A platform. The crane boom fell uncontrollably, striking platform handrails and the motor vessel stern. Investigation revealed a seized bearing in the boom cable bridle sheave assembly caused the sheave pin to wear down approximately 1/2 inch. The cable cut through the Nylatron sheave material until it contacted the hardened bearing race, then cut through the sheave side, ultimately failing under slow strain. A small amount of hydraulic fluid entered the Gulf of America from broken hoses and fittings. No injuries occurred. Damage totaled $125,000.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - West Delta Block 73
incident__event__summary: On 17 February 2023 at approximately 0315 hours, a gas leak occurred on a 12-inch gas pipeline riser (PSN 759) approximately 5-7 feet above the water line at the WD 73AT production platform. The ESD was activated, shutting down the entire facility. All 26 personnel on board mustered upwind at the quarters structure and sheltered in place due to severe weather conditions (5-8 foot seas, 20-30 knot winds with gusts to 40 knots). The pipeline was bled down from 1080 psi to 0 psi. A light non-recoverable sheen (2.56 miles long, 300 feet wide) was observed during overflight. Investigation determined the leak was caused by inadequate preventative maintenance, with repairs identified in a 2015 Level 2 survey never completed, and inadequate inspection methods (drone survey on 15 Feb 2023 insufficient, no intelligent PIG inspection conducted).

incident__context__region: Gulf of America, West Delta Block 90
incident__event__summary: On April 21, 2025, at approximately 19:30hrs CDT, the West Delta 90 B platform experienced a loss of well control during Plug and Abandonment operations on well B020. While pumping fluid down the tubing to equalize pressure across a downhole plug, approximately 20 barrels of oil and gas were released from a leak at the starter head flange and 10-3/4 x 20 surface casing annulus. The uncontrolled release led to full platform evacuation. The incident resulted from a breach at the spooling head and failure within the tubing string that enabled hydraulic communication between tubing and annulus. Contributing factors included inadequate pre-job planning, failure to incorporate historical well data showing prior leakage issues, and lack of hazard assessment before continuing operations after initial leak detection.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Walker Ridge Block 51
incident__event__summary: On January 7, 2023, during drilling operations on the Transocean Deepwater Atlas drillship, a 6-5/8 inch drill pipe joint weighing 60.78 lbs/ft was dropped 58 feet onto the pipe skate when the Pipe Laydown System (PLS) failed to maintain grip. The PLS only engaged the roller jaw while the centralizer and gripper arms did not fully grip due to a hydraulic leak. The Assistant Driller could not visually confirm proper grip due to obstructed view and relied solely on the control screen which gave no indication of the partial grip or unintended movement. All personnel were in the safe step back area and no injuries occurred.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - West Delta Block 31
incident__event__summary: On 19 November 2022 at approximately 0430 hours, motor vessel Jacob Gerald allided into West Delta 31 Platform E-DRL while transporting personnel. The captain dozed off before impact. The vessel was traveling at cruising speed of 8 knots when the captain fell asleep. A production operator aboard the vessel reported back soreness and was transported to an onshore medical facility, later released to full duty (first aid case). The allision caused damage to several structural members. The platform was shut in and the damaged area barricaded. Engineering analysis determined the platform was safe to man with wave heights below 20 feet. Total repair costs remain unknown but not expected to exceed $25K.

While performing routine maintenance on a centrifugal pump, a worker removed a coupling guard. The pump was inadvertently started during troubleshooting, causing the rotating shaft to catch the worker's glove. The worker suffered a hand injury.

During crane lifting operations on an offshore deck, the load swung unexpectedly due to wind gusts. A rigger attempted to steady the load manually and suffered a shoulder injury. Pre-job briefing did not address wind limits and spotter coverage was insufficient.

incident__event__top_event: Worker entered confined space without permit
incident__event__summary: A worker entered a confined space to inspect a vessel without completing the permit. Gas testing was not performed and ventilation was not set up. The worker experienced dizziness and required medical evaluation.
A pressurized hose ruptured during line flushing, striking a technician and causing lacerations.
A gas leak occurred at the north valve station due to severe corrosion on the main flange. The leak was detected by the Fire & Gas Detection system, triggering an automatic ESD System shutdown.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, East Breaks Block 699
incident__event__summary: On November 16, 2022, at 05:11 AM, approximately twelve barrels of Synthetic Based Mud (SBM) were discharged into the Gulf of Mexico from the Diamond Offshore drillship West Vela during exploration drilling operations at 25,000 feet depth. A saltwater kick of fifty-eight barrels occurred below the base of salt, causing the Managed Pressure Drilling (MPD) system Surface Back Pressure to exceed 1,300 psi, triggering Pressure Relief Valves that were lined up to discharge overboard instead of to the flowline. The well was successfully shut in with shut-in drill pipe pressure of 1,600 psi, and the kick was circulated out. The MPD system was not designed to control influxes greater than five barrels.
Stub incident for testing purposes.
